name: Auth Advanced
description: OAuth, OIDC, PKCE, SSO, and enterprise auth patterns for senior developers.

cards.front: What is OAuth 2.0, what are its four roles, and why does it exist?
cards.back:
OAuth 2.0 is an authorization delegation framework, not an authentication protocol.
• The four roles: Resource Owner (user), Client (your app), Authorization Server (issues tokens), Resource Server (the API).
• It exists so users can grant limited access to their resources without sharing credentials.
• OAuth replaces the anti-pattern of apps collecting usernames/passwords for third-party services.
• It uses access tokens (opaque or JWT) instead of credentials to authorize API requests.
• Common mistake: treating OAuth as an identity/authentication protocol. It only handles authorization.
• The spec is RFC 6749. Real-world implementations (Google, GitHub, Okta) each add their own extensions.
• As a senior dev, know that OAuth is the foundation layer; OIDC adds identity on top.

cards.front: Walk through the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow step by step.
cards.back:
1. Client redirects user to the Authorization Server's /authorize endpoint with client_id, redirect_uri, response_type=code, scope, and state.
• 2. User authenticates and consents on the Authorization Server.
• 3. Authorization Server redirects back to redirect_uri with an authorization code and the state parameter.
• 4. Client validates the state parameter matches what it sent (CSRF protection).
• 5. Client sends the authorization code + client_secret to the /token endpoint (back-channel POST).
• 6. Authorization Server returns an access_token (and optionally a refresh_token).
• The code is short-lived (typically 30-60 seconds) and single-use.
• The back-channel exchange keeps the access token out of the browser URL and history.
• The state parameter is critical: without it, an attacker can inject their own authorization code.
• This flow is preferred for server-side apps because the client_secret stays on the server.

cards.front: What is PKCE, why do SPAs and mobile apps need it, and how does it work?
cards.back:
PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange, RFC 7636) prevents authorization code interception attacks.
• SPAs cannot securely store a client_secret, so the back-channel exchange in standard Auth Code flow is vulnerable.
• The client generates a random code_verifier (43-128 chars, cryptographically random).
• It hashes the verifier with SHA-256 to produce a code_challenge, sent with the /authorize request.
• When exchanging the code at /token, the client sends the original code_verifier instead of a client_secret.
• The server hashes the verifier and compares it to the stored challenge. Only the original client can complete the exchange.
• This replaces the now-deprecated Implicit flow, which exposed tokens in URL fragments.
• Libraries like oidc-client-ts and Auth0 SPA SDK handle PKCE automatically.
• As of OAuth 2.1 (draft), PKCE is required for ALL clients, not just public ones.
• Always use S256 (SHA-256) for the challenge method. The plain method offers no real security.

cards.front: What is OpenID Connect (OIDC) and how does it add identity on top of OAuth 2.0?
cards.back:
OIDC is a thin identity layer built on top of OAuth 2.0 that standardizes authentication.
• OAuth alone answers 'what can this app access?' OIDC adds 'who is the user?'
• It introduces the ID token, a JWT containing user identity claims (sub, email, name, etc.).
• The openid scope triggers the OIDC flow; additional scopes like profile and email request more claims.
• OIDC defines a /userinfo endpoint to fetch additional claims using the access token.
• It standardizes a discovery document at /.well-known/openid-configuration with all endpoint URLs, supported scopes, and signing keys.
• The ID token is signed (and optionally encrypted) so the client can verify authenticity without another server call.
• Key providers: Okta, Auth0, Azure AD, Keycloak, Google. All follow the same spec.
• Common mistake: sending the ID token to your API as a bearer token. Use the access token for API access; the ID token is for the client.
• OIDC also defines standard flows for logout (front-channel, back-channel, RP-initiated).

cards.front: What are the differences between ID tokens and access tokens, and what mistakes do developers commonly make with each?
cards.back:
The ID token proves WHO the user is; the access token proves WHAT the user can access.
• ID tokens are always JWTs with standard claims (sub, iss, aud, exp, iat, nonce). They are consumed by the client app.
• Access tokens can be opaque strings or JWTs. They are consumed by the resource server (API).
• The ID token audience (aud) is the client_id. The access token audience is the API identifier.
• Mistake 1: Sending the ID token as a Bearer token to your API. APIs should validate access tokens.
• Mistake 2: Storing sensitive permissions in the ID token. Permissions belong in the access token or should be fetched server-side.
• Mistake 3: Not validating the nonce in the ID token, which opens replay attacks.
• ID tokens should be validated once at login and then discarded or stored briefly. Access tokens are sent with every API call.
• Access tokens are typically short-lived (5-60 min). ID tokens have similar expiry but are not refreshed; you re-authenticate.
• In Auth0/Okta, access tokens for your own APIs can be JWTs with custom claims and scopes.

cards.front: How does social login (federated identity) like 'Sign in with Google' work under the hood?
cards.back:
Social login uses OIDC (or OAuth + proprietary extensions) with the social provider as the Identity Provider (IdP).
• Your app registers as a client with the provider (Google Console, Facebook Developer Portal) and receives a client_id and client_secret.
• On login, your app redirects to the provider's /authorize endpoint. The user authenticates there, not on your site.
• After consent, the provider redirects back with an authorization code. You exchange it for tokens.
• The ID token or /userinfo response gives you the user's email, name, and a stable sub identifier.
• You map the provider's sub + issuer to a user record in your database (account linking).
• Account linking is the hard part: users may sign up with email, then try Google, creating duplicate accounts. Handle this with email-based linking and verification.
• Libraries: passport.js (Node), next-auth/Auth.js, Spring Security OAuth2 Client.
• Security consideration: always verify the email is verified (email_verified claim) before trusting it for account linking.
• For multiple providers, consider an identity broker like Auth0 or Keycloak to normalize the differences.

cards.front: What are OAuth scopes and consent, and how should you design scopes for your own APIs?
cards.back:
Scopes define the specific permissions a client is requesting from the user. Consent is the user approving those scopes.
• Scopes appear in the /authorize request (scope=openid profile email read:documents).
• The consent screen shows users exactly what they are granting. Overly broad scopes erode user trust.
• Design principle: use resource:action format (e.g., repos:read, users:write, billing:admin).
• Keep scopes coarse enough to be understandable but fine enough to enforce least privilege.
• Common mistake: creating one god scope like api:full_access. This defeats the purpose of scoped authorization.
• First-party apps (your own SPA calling your API) can skip the consent screen since the user already trusts you.
• Third-party apps should always show consent and support incremental authorization (requesting new scopes only when needed).
• On the API side, validate scopes in middleware: check the access token's scope claim before allowing the operation.
• Scope design is an API design decision. Document your scopes in your API reference the same way you document endpoints.

cards.front: What are the main token refresh strategies, and how do you implement them in a frontend app?
cards.back:
Refresh tokens allow obtaining new access tokens without re-authenticating the user.
• Strategy 1: Axios/fetch interceptors. Intercept 401 responses, call /token with the refresh token, retry the original request. Queue concurrent requests during refresh.
• Strategy 2: Proactive refresh. Check the access token's exp claim before each request; refresh when it is within a buffer window (e.g., 30 seconds before expiry).
• Strategy 3: Silent refresh via hidden iframe (legacy). Loads the /authorize endpoint in an iframe using prompt=none. Broken by third-party cookie blocking in modern browsers.
• Refresh Token Rotation: each refresh returns a new refresh token and invalidates the old one. If a stolen token is reused, the server detects the replay and revokes the entire family.
• For SPAs, store refresh tokens in memory (not localStorage). Use the BFF (Backend for Frontend) pattern to keep tokens server-side behind httpOnly cookies.
• Libraries like @auth0/auth0-react and msal-browser handle refresh logic, token caching, and race conditions.
• Always handle the case where the refresh token itself is expired or revoked: redirect to login.
• Set reasonable lifetimes: access tokens 5-15 min, refresh tokens hours to days depending on sensitivity.

cards.front: What is SAML, when will you encounter it, and how does it compare to OIDC?
cards.back:
SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) is an XML-based SSO protocol dominant in enterprise environments.
• You will encounter SAML when integrating with corporate identity providers (Active Directory Federation Services, Okta, Ping Identity).
• SAML uses assertions (XML documents) instead of tokens (JWTs). Assertions contain authentication statements, attribute statements, and authorization statements.
• The flow: Service Provider (your app) redirects to Identity Provider (IdP). IdP authenticates the user and POSTs a signed SAML assertion back via the user's browser.
• SAML is heavier than OIDC: XML parsing, XML signature validation, certificate management, and complex metadata exchange.
• OIDC is REST/JSON-based and better suited for SPAs, mobile apps, and modern architectures.
• SAML is still required for many enterprise B2B integrations. If an enterprise customer says 'we use SAML,' you need to support it.
• Most auth providers (Auth0, Okta, Azure AD) act as a bridge: accept SAML from the corporate IdP, expose OIDC to your app.
• Key vulnerability: XML Signature Wrapping attacks. Always use a well-tested SAML library (e.g., node-saml, Spring Security SAML).
• As a tech lead, push for OIDC when possible, but budget for SAML support in any B2B SaaS product.

cards.front: How does Single Sign-On (SSO) work across multiple applications?
cards.back:
SSO lets users authenticate once and access multiple applications without re-entering credentials.
• It works via a centralized Identity Provider (IdP) that maintains a session (usually a cookie on the IdP domain).
• When App A redirects to the IdP, the user logs in. The IdP sets a session cookie on its own domain.
• When App B redirects to the same IdP, the IdP sees the existing session cookie and issues tokens immediately without prompting for credentials.
• Protocol layer: OIDC uses prompt=none for silent auth checks. SAML uses IsPassive=true.
• SSO logout is the hard part. OIDC defines front-channel logout (iframes pinging each app's logout URL) and back-channel logout (server-to-server notifications).
• Front-channel logout is unreliable (blocked iframes, third-party cookies). Back-channel logout is more robust but requires each app to expose a logout webhook.
• Common architecture: all apps in a domain use a shared IdP (Okta, Azure AD, Keycloak). Each app is a separate OIDC client.
• Tradeoff: SSO is a single point of failure. If the IdP goes down, no one can log in anywhere.
• For senior devs: plan for session duration mismatches between apps and the IdP. Define a clear SSO timeout policy.

cards.front: What is the difference between RBAC and ABAC, and when should you use each?
cards.back:
RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) assigns permissions to roles, then roles to users. ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) evaluates policies against attributes at runtime.
• RBAC example: user has role 'editor', editor role can 'create:article' and 'edit:article'.
• ABAC example: allow if user.department === resource.department AND user.clearanceLevel >= resource.requiredClearance AND time is within business hours.
• RBAC is simpler to implement, audit, and explain. Use it when your permission model has a manageable number of distinct roles.
• ABAC is more flexible and fine-grained. Use it when decisions depend on dynamic context (resource ownership, time, location, risk score).
• In practice, most systems use RBAC as the foundation and layer in attribute-based checks for specific cases (e.g., 'editors can edit articles, but only in their own department').
• Implementation: store roles in the JWT or fetch from an authorization service. For ABAC, use a policy engine like Open Policy Agent (OPA) or Cerbos.
• Common mistake: role explosion. If you have 50+ roles, your RBAC model is too granular and you need ABAC or permission-based access control.
• In frontend apps, roles drive UI visibility (show/hide features). But ALWAYS enforce authorization on the server. Client-side checks are UX, not security.
• Google Zanzibar (used by Google Drive) is a relationship-based model (ReBAC) that handles 'user X has role Y on resource Z' at scale. Inspired SpiceDB and Authzed.

cards.front: How should you implement authentication in a React SPA, including AuthContext, protected routes, and loading states?
cards.back:
Create an AuthProvider context that wraps your app and exposes user, isAuthenticated, isLoading, login, and logout.
• On mount, the AuthProvider checks for an existing session (e.g., calls /api/me or checks the auth SDK's getUser()). While checking, isLoading is true.
• Critical: render a loading spinner or skeleton while isLoading is true. Never flash the login page before the auth check completes.
• Protected routes: create a RequireAuth wrapper component that checks isAuthenticated and redirects to /login if false.
• With React Router v6: <Route element={<RequireAuth><Dashboard /></RequireAuth>} />.
• Store tokens in memory (a ref or closure), not localStorage. Use httpOnly cookies or the BFF pattern for refresh tokens.
• Handle token refresh in an Axios interceptor: on 401, refresh the token, retry the request, and queue concurrent requests during the refresh.
• Auth libraries that handle this well: @auth0/auth0-react, @azure/msal-react, next-auth (for Next.js), and oidc-client-ts.
• Common mistake: putting auth logic in every component. Centralize it in the provider and expose hooks like useAuth().
• For role-based UI: create a usePermission('edit:article') hook that reads from the auth context. Keep the check declarative.

cards.front: How do you implement authentication in Next.js with middleware, server components, and API routes?
cards.back:
Next.js has three auth enforcement layers: middleware, server components, and API route handlers.
• Middleware (middleware.ts at project root): runs on the Edge before every request. Check for a session cookie; redirect to /login if absent. Use NextResponse.redirect() or NextResponse.next().
• Middleware is ideal for broad route protection (e.g., protect everything under /dashboard/*) but cannot do heavy DB lookups on the Edge.
• Server Components: use cookies() or headers() from next/headers to read the session cookie. Call your auth verification function and conditionally render or redirect.
• API Routes (app/api/*/route.ts): validate the session cookie or Bearer token in each handler. Return 401/403 for unauthorized requests.
• Auth.js (NextAuth v5) integrates all three layers: provides auth() helper for server components, middleware exports, and API route handlers.
• For JWTs, verify the token server-side in middleware using jose library (Edge-compatible, no Node.js crypto dependency).
• The iron-session library provides encrypted cookie-based sessions that work across all Next.js rendering modes.
• Common mistake: only protecting pages in middleware but forgetting to protect API routes. Attackers call APIs directly.
• Architecture choice: server-side sessions in encrypted cookies (stateless) vs. database sessions (revocable but require a DB lookup on every request).

cards.front: What is passwordless authentication and how do passkeys (WebAuthn) work?
cards.back:
Passwordless auth eliminates passwords entirely using cryptographic credentials, biometrics, or magic links.
• WebAuthn (Web Authentication API) is the W3C standard that enables passkeys. It uses public-key cryptography.
• Registration: the browser generates a key pair. The private key stays on the device (secure enclave, TPM, or cloud-synced). The public key is sent to the server.
• Authentication: the server sends a challenge. The device signs it with the private key (unlocked via biometric or PIN). The server verifies with the stored public key.
• Passkeys are phishing-resistant: the credential is bound to the origin (RP ID). A fake site on a different domain cannot trigger the credential.
• Synced passkeys (Apple Keychain, Google Password Manager, 1Password) solve the device-loss problem by syncing across devices.
• Server-side libraries: SimpleWebAuthn (Node), py_webauthn (Python), java-webauthn-server (Java).
• Implementation: call navigator.credentials.create() for registration and navigator.credentials.get() for login. Both return attestation/assertion objects to verify server-side.
• Tradeoff: passkeys are excellent UX but require fallback flows (magic link, recovery codes) for users without passkey-capable devices.
• As a tech lead: passkeys are the future. Start offering them as an option alongside passwords, then make them primary.

cards.front: How does authentication and authorization work in a microservices architecture?
cards.back:
In microservices, auth is typically handled at the API gateway layer, with token validation distributed across services.
• Pattern 1: API Gateway (Kong, AWS API Gateway, Envoy) validates the access token on every inbound request and forwards verified claims (user ID, roles, scopes) as headers to downstream services.
• Pattern 2: Each service validates the JWT independently using the IdP's public keys (fetched from the JWKS endpoint). No gateway dependency, but every service needs validation logic.
• Service-to-service auth: use the OAuth Client Credentials flow. Each service has its own client_id/client_secret and requests a token scoped to the target service's API.
• Never pass the user's access token between internal services. It may have scopes or audience restrictions that do not apply to internal calls.
• Token validation must check: signature, exp, iss, aud, and scopes/permissions. Use a validated library (jose, jsonwebtoken, nimbus-jose-jwt).
• For authorization, centralize policy evaluation with Open Policy Agent (OPA) or a dedicated authorization service rather than spreading if/else checks across services.
• Propagate the user context (user ID, tenant ID) via headers or a request-scoped token, not by decoding the JWT in every service.
• Tradeoff: JWTs are stateless (no revocation until expiry) vs. opaque tokens with introspection (revocable but require a network call to the auth server).
• Keep access token lifetimes very short (5 min) in microservices to limit the blast radius of a compromised token.

cards.front: Which security headers matter for authentication, and why should you configure CSP, HSTS, and X-Frame-Options?
cards.back:
Security headers form a defense-in-depth layer that mitigates token theft and session hijacking.
• Content-Security-Policy (CSP): restricts what scripts, styles, and connections the browser allows. Prevents inline script injection that could steal tokens from memory or cookies.
• Set a strict CSP: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; connect-src 'self' https://your-api.com; frame-ancestors 'none'.
• Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS): forces HTTPS. Set max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload. Prevents SSL stripping attacks that could intercept auth cookies.
• X-Frame-Options: DENY (or CSP frame-ancestors 'none') prevents your app from being embedded in iframes, blocking clickjacking attacks that trick users into clicking hidden auth buttons.
• X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff prevents browsers from MIME-sniffing responses, blocking attacks where a malicious file is interpreted as executable.
• Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin prevents leaking auth tokens or session IDs in URL query parameters via the Referer header.
• Set-Cookie flags: always use Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite=Lax (or Strict) on session and auth cookies.
• Cache-Control: no-store on authenticated responses prevents sensitive data from being cached by browsers or proxies.
• In Next.js, set headers in next.config.js securityHeaders array or in middleware. In Express, use the helmet middleware.
• Audit your headers with securityheaders.com and Mozilla Observatory.

cards.front: What are session fixation and session hijacking attacks, and how do you prevent them?
cards.back:
Session fixation: attacker sets a known session ID on the victim's browser before they log in. After the victim authenticates, the attacker uses the same session ID to access their account.
• Prevention: always regenerate the session ID immediately after successful authentication. In Express: req.session.regenerate(). In most frameworks, this is a one-line call.
• Session hijacking: attacker steals an active session ID via XSS, network sniffing, or malware and impersonates the user.
• Hijacking via XSS: if a script can read document.cookie, it can exfiltrate the session cookie. Prevention: HttpOnly flag on cookies blocks JavaScript access.
• Hijacking via network sniffing: unencrypted HTTP exposes cookies in transit. Prevention: HTTPS everywhere plus the Secure cookie flag and HSTS.
• Hijacking via malware/browser extensions: out of your control at the app level, but short session lifetimes and re-authentication for sensitive actions limit the window.
• Additional defenses: bind the session to the user's IP or user-agent fingerprint and invalidate it if they change. This adds friction but limits lateral movement.
• Implement absolute session timeouts (e.g., 8 hours) and idle timeouts (e.g., 30 minutes) regardless of activity.
• For high-security apps, use step-up authentication: require a fresh login or MFA challenge before sensitive operations (password change, payment).
• Monitor for anomalies: concurrent sessions from different geolocations, session reuse from new IPs.

cards.front: What are the main token revocation strategies, and what are the tradeoffs of each?
cards.back:
JWTs are stateless by design, which makes revocation inherently difficult. Every strategy involves a tradeoff between security, performance, and complexity.
• Strategy 1: Short-lived access tokens (5-15 min). The token naturally expires quickly. Combined with refresh token rotation, this limits the window of abuse without any server-side state.
• Strategy 2: Token blacklist/denylist. Store revoked token IDs (jti claim) in a fast store like Redis. Check the blacklist on every request. Adds latency and a single point of failure.
• Strategy 3: Refresh token rotation with reuse detection. Each refresh issues a new refresh token and invalidates the old. If a revoked refresh token is reused, revoke the entire token family (all descendants).
• Strategy 4: Token versioning. Store a tokenVersion counter on the user record. Increment it on logout/password change. Reject access tokens whose version does not match.
• Strategy 5: Opaque tokens with introspection (RFC 7662). The API calls the auth server's /introspect endpoint to check if the token is active. Fully revocable but adds a network call per request.
• For OAuth, RFC 7009 defines a /revoke endpoint that clients can call to revoke refresh or access tokens.
• In practice, combine short expiry + refresh rotation + a lightweight event system (e.g., publish revocation events to a pub/sub channel that API instances subscribe to).
• Common mistake: relying solely on long-lived JWTs with no revocation mechanism. A compromised 24-hour JWT gives an attacker a full day.
• As a tech lead, document your revocation strategy in your architecture decision records. It is a security-critical design choice.

cards.front: What should you test in your authentication system, and what tools help with auth security testing?
cards.back:
Auth testing spans functional tests, integration tests, and security-specific tests.
• Functional: test login/logout flows, token refresh, session expiry, role-based access (authorized users see content, unauthorized get 403), and edge cases (expired tokens, malformed tokens, missing headers).
• Integration: test the full OAuth/OIDC flow with your IdP in a staging environment. Verify redirect URIs, scope handling, and token validation end-to-end.
• Security test: attempt to access protected endpoints without a token, with an expired token, with a token signed by a different key, and with a token for a different audience.
• Test for common vulnerabilities: IDOR (accessing another user's resources by changing IDs), privilege escalation (changing your role claim client-side), and token reuse after logout.
• Tools: OWASP ZAP (automated security scanning, free), Burp Suite (intercepting proxy for manual testing), and Postman/Insomnia for crafting auth requests.
• For automated tests, use Playwright or Cypress to test the full login flow in the browser, including social login with test accounts.
• JWT-specific testing: use jwt.io to decode tokens and verify claims. Use a library to craft tokens with tampered claims and verify your API rejects them.
• Dependency scanning: run npm audit or Snyk to check your auth libraries for known vulnerabilities.
• Penetration testing: for production systems, schedule regular pentests that specifically target authentication and session management (OWASP Testing Guide sections V4.05-V4.06).
• Include auth tests in CI/CD. A broken auth flow should block deployment.

cards.front: What is zero trust architecture, and how does it change the way you think about authentication and authorization?
cards.back:
Zero trust operates on the principle 'never trust, always verify.' No request is trusted based on network location alone.
• Traditional model: once inside the corporate network (VPN), you are trusted. Zero trust: every request is authenticated and authorized regardless of origin.
• Core principles: verify explicitly (authenticate every request), use least-privilege access (minimal permissions for minimal time), and assume breach (design as if the network is already compromised).
• Implementation: every service-to-service call carries a verifiable identity (mTLS certificates or signed tokens). No service gets implicit trust from being 'inside' the network.
• Device trust: zero trust evaluates the device posture (OS patch level, disk encryption, MDM compliance) before granting access. Tools: Zscaler, Cloudflare Access, Google BeyondCorp.
• Continuous verification: do not just check auth at the front door. Re-evaluate risk signals throughout the session (location changes, anomalous behavior, time-based policies).
• For frontend developers: zero trust means your BFF or API gateway may enforce step-up auth, device checks, or conditional access policies that you need to handle gracefully in the UI.
• Microsegmentation: instead of one big trusted network, each service has its own access policy. An attacker who compromises one service cannot move laterally.
• Architecture pattern: use an identity-aware proxy (like Cloudflare Access or Pomerium) that authenticates users before traffic even reaches your application.
• Zero trust is not a product you buy; it is a design philosophy. Adopt it incrementally: start with strong identity, enforce MFA, then layer on device trust and continuous evaluation.
